item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I enjoyed this take of alt history; the idea to begin with is starting on the right foot and the article doesn't ever trip over itself, imo. The character-speak is well done and imagining the last moments of those killed in the containment breach is pretty terrifying, especially if they got a good glimpse of Robo-George.

My only nitpick is the last line, "SCP-2776: …and by the grace of God Almighty, I will be the Law." I understand that compositionally it is the creepiness stinger, and it does that well, but by equating itself with the Law, Robo-George thematically betrays its immediately-previous line ("…laws, not kings."), as well as the values he would have been fighting the British over in the first place.

item.title: Re: typo or...?
item.content:encoded: The "which" might be referring to the automaton rather than Washington. The wording is on the ambiguous side here given the sentence structure.

item.title: typo or...?
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2776 is a humanoid automaton bearing the aged likeness of deceased senior British army officer George Washington, which held the office of President of the United States from April 30th, 1789, to March 4th, 1797. For more information about the origin of SCP-2776, please see Addendum 2776.3.

Why is it not
"[…]deceased senior British army officer George Washington, WHO held the office of President of the United States from April 30th […]"

?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Do you happen to be from Roanoke? That’s actually where I’m from so this made me really happy to see someone reference my home city!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It’s a county in Virginia, I’m from there actually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wenss-Newton electromagnet? Isn't Wenss supposed to be Weiss?

item.title: Junior Staff Post - Crosslink Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer expired - edits implemented.
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Proposed Edits
- Proposed Edits

The proposed edit is to link this article to the Marshall, Carter, and Dark hub. The article specifically mentions the GoI in the Discovery addendum.

Editing this:

SCP-2776 was first discovered during a 2007 raid on a Marshall, Carter and Dark distributor acting out of Roanoke, Virginia.

To this:

SCP-2776 was first discovered during a 2007 raid on a Marshall, Carter and Dark distributor acting out of Roanoke, Virginia.

item.title: Admin Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Noting that a recent edit by StevenThePotato was reverted, as it added additional words that completely changed the meaning of a sentence, which is considered a stylistic edit and as such requires permission from the original author or staff to be made.

Editors, if you're not fixing (unintentionally mistaken) grammar, mechanics, or formatting, please obtain permission prior to making changes to a page. This is noted in the Site Rules.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Funnily enough, Automatron was the name of the first Fallout 4 DLC which enabled you to build various robots and death machines. Theoretically, you could make a very crude "Robo-Washington" using the Automatron DLC. The more you know!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If the mainlist title isn't "The United States of Automatons" by the morning, I am sueing everybody.

But in all seriousness, +1, I enjoyed it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like, from the word go, the Brad Neely song instantly came into my head. Didn't so much as glance at the comments, I swear.

…at least someone waited until series 3 to add this, because I can't help but think the series 1 version of this article would be sure to include this motherfucker's thirty goddamn [REDACTED]

item.title: Similarities
item.content:encoded:
Is there any relationship between this and SCP-186? The gas that makes cells unable to cease life functions is very similar and the tumor field are similar to some of the weapons from it. Is it possible the person who orchestrated the events of SCP-186 is affiliated with the creation of robo-washington?

EDIT: Just saw the post above mine, nvm

item.title: Independence!
item.content:encoded: Just mentioning this one for everyone today.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Biological material covered in the mist did not seem to be able to cease life functions until the mist had dissipated.

Yep, sounds like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are the signatures "J" and "Durand" references to SCP-186?

item.title: Re: Spread, Spread, Delaware
item.content:encoded: I am in tears

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree very much to this post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey kaktus? Are his teeth supposed to be two hundred years older than him? "…and mid-16th century dental prosthesis." — in a mid-18th century 'bot? I'm guessing typo :)

Other than it going a bit level 9000 at the end there, I like it. My +1 would be stronger if you hadn't detailed what happened when he went beserk and had just left it as "camera footage unrecoverable" or something. But +1 all the same!

item.title: Re: Spread, Spread, Delaware
item.content:encoded: That video popped into my mind as soon as I saw the list in the containment procedures. Then I got to the incident report and I knew it wasn't a coincidence. Well played.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2776: …and by the grace of God Almighty, I will be the Law.

That line… *shivers*… I didn't expect an alternative history SCP to give me the kind of chills I've come to expect from the more horrific entries on this site. Superb. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd throw Robocop in there as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Firstly, +1.

I was fascinated enough to do some research. This is what I found.

John Toliver was a real person who was in his late twenties and early thirties in the 1750s (the Battle of the Monongahela was in 1755). He fathered the "Five Toliver Brothers" of Wilkes County, North Carolina. I have no idea what that means. Little other than his name and his offspring are known. Check it out: http://www.moonzstuff.com/toliver/john1725.html

George Washington fought for the British at the Battle of the Monongahela, but was not wounded there. In fact, he rallied and reordered the British forces in retreat after another Native American ambush had sent the already fleeing and largely wounded soldiers into a panic. Say this was a cover story employed by MC&D. If he had been wounded in battle, how would he have escaped alive?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a really engaging scip, but the incident details kinda spoil it for me. I think you could delete everything after the end of Addendum 2776.3, leave Addendum 2776.4 classified and inaccessible, and this would be pretty much perfect. The fact that this scip was our war-winning general and first president is more than enough to sell this article. The kind of things I imagined happening in the event of a breach were mostly better than the actual things that happened. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now that I see the similarities, my downvote stands even firmer. It's even less original than I had thought.

No, I'm not bothered by reintroducing Durand, the concept of archeotech, or sharing canon between two scips. It happens all the time, I've done it myself.

I am bothered that the two entries are almost identical in terms of format and story, with nothing new except the aforementioned quirkiness of introducing us to "Washingterminator."

So whether you intended sell me action figure, you're still selling me an action figure, and now I see its one that's mimicking an earlier, better scip. I'll stick with the original, thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a shame SCP-1776 is already taken…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was one of the worst Star Trek episodes, "The Savage Curtain" (TOS 3x22).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why not have bots for every country and state in the world? And have them fight one another in a massive tournament.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the concept of Robo-George, but the weird containment breach thing and end interview killed it for me. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We need a Button Gwinnet bot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreeing with the sentiment here, I would be more thrilled to see this concept applied on other national "founders" (China's Dr. Sun, India's Gandhi, Singapore's LKY, Vietnam's Ho etc.). Although since the author is likely familiar with US himself, it can't be helped and I will accept a Washington bot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, Liberty Prime X Motorized Patriot? I feel like both the concept of an automaton version of G. Washington and an Patriotic american killing machine are kind of a cliché. I would like it better if another historic figure had been used.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I certainly wouldn't call this skip "perfect;" I feel like you could play around with the word choices in the Incident Report (2776.4) a bit to see if it improves the tone, and give the whole page one more look-through to take care of some grammatical errors ("woman" instead of "women," etc.)

Some of the portions of the Incident Report also feel a bit…I don't know. Overkill-y? But this might be more due to personal taste.

But I am still giving it a +1, because the concept is pretty cool in itself, and because something about that last interview gave me the jeebies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it a lot. +1

Only complaint I have is that you said "liquid mist" at the end when describing 2776 in murder-mode. Sounds kind of awkward/redundant to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Zombie Washington from Tales of the Drunken Paladin approves. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't get why information on the high energy mode is expunged. I mean, you really don't want the personnel to get lax on this one, so I see no reason not to scare them a bit.

Other than that, +1.

SCP-2776 is not to come in contact with SCP-50-AE-J under any circumstances.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good catch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You still have the bit about how a Keter upgrade was ruled for in Addendum 4 though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, that was leftover from an earlier, more aggressive version of this, and I can't justify it to myself anymore. Editing appropriately.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mixed feelings over this concept (since the same base concept could easily be carried over to any national founder if you change the context accordingly). Tentative upvote, largely due to the Cool factor and details involved (and the fact I am not well-versed in US history to really fact-check the backstory).

I'm still not sure why it's Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You wound me. I was introduced to that song after I described the concept in staff chat. If you had looked, you would have noticed that the skip shares far more in common with this than that video.

In other words, I've no intention to sell you action figures.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm somewhat disappointed he's neither six foot twenty nor fuckin' killin' for fun, but otherwise excellent. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No? Because you pointed to it pretty succinctly with your link to that Washington song. I was just going to ignore the scip without a vote until I saw that. Then I was like "Oh, one of those scips."

This isn't a scip that's trying to tell a story or imply there's some deeper meaning to the War of Independence. This is Saturday Morning Cartoon nonsense trying to make stuff "EXTREME" to sell me action figures. And that would be fine, if that were the point. But it's not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quirky? I don't think I follow.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm going to downvote this one. It's a clean write-up, and it deserves the upvotes it gets. But I feel like it's shouting "quirky!" at me, and is insubstantial otherwise.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
any by the grace of God Almighty

Shouldn't that be "And" by the grace of God?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Motorized Patriots, and yes, this does kinda give off that vibe.
"The Lord judges; I act."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You use "automatron" when I think you mean "automaton" and "automatronic" when I think you mean "animatronic."

Automatron and automatronic aren't real words, as far as I can tell.

Other than that, good stuff. But now you need to cross test him with a giant mutant zombie Abe Lincoln.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me of the Mechanized(?) Patriots from Bioshock Infinite. Did enough to keep what I liked about them (super weapon-y, spouting random bits of patriotic mumbo jumbo) but changed it enough to give it something nice. Like emotional androids who didn't know they were androids. I'm now going to make this my default conspiracy to spout off instead of the secret lizard overlords. All in all, 9.5/10.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Speaking as a Brit, thanks MTF for preventing Robo-Washington from making angry landfall and forcing us to pull Cyborg-Wellington out of mothballs!
Ooh, actually, I want to write a tale about that now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So Washington was a robot by the time he gained the presidency. I can dig. You seem to have confused the word "automaton" and "animatronic", and there's a "Once you have corpse" but otherwise, this is pretty neato.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Roanoke, Virgina

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What's all the stuff about Roanoke, man? Didn't that fail IRL?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No no, not even close. Current day, current reality, United States won the Revolution. Because of this, Durand's Washington didn't need to kick into "murderdeathkill" mode, and was only recovered by the Foundation a long time after the fact.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: See, this is where you lose me. This reads like it takes place in a parallel universe where the Revolution failed and Robo-Washington is a weird-ass relic that British Foundation now has to deal with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Robo-Washington didn't fail, though. That primary activation mode, and the accompanying "I Am The Law", was a failsafe built in if the States were going to lose the Revolution.

He didn't need it, though, because he won the war.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Meh. I can dig alt-universe fiction on the mainlist (because it's basically my whole shtick), but, without an in-universe reason for this taking place somewhere Roanoke didn't fail and the Revolution did, I have to novote.

I mean, as an American, I have to love Robo-Washington, but as an American, I can't upvote a Robo-Washington who failed. Because we all know that Robo-Washington never fails.

You know what would get my upvote, though? Robo-Benedict Arnold.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No-voting. It has a lot of stuff that I like, the robotic Washington, the incident, but it never all adds up together. It never really fully clicks. It's close, very close, but it doesn't tie together at the end and it ending with Washington becoming a death machine, while cool, is also anti-climactic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was a thoroughly entertaining read. Robots, lasers, alternative history, this skip has it all. As I said in chat, the description does drag a bit, but the containment procedures set the article up nicely and the addenda really make for a nice finish.

+1 for Robo-Washington.

item.title: Spread, Spread, Delaware
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to everybody who checked this out in chat, specifically Decibelle, Dr Leonard, Conwell, and a handful of others. Much appreciated!

This is all I have to say about this.

Hope you guys like it!
